banner: Week 7 · Angle Pairs — Supplementary, Complementary & Vertical
essential_question: Two angles can be paired up four different ways — which pairing is guaranteed true no matter what?
objective: Define and identify adjacent, complementary, supplementary, and vertical angle pairs, and compute an unknown angle's measure from its paired partner.

content.prompt: Draw two straight lines crossing through one point. You now have four angles. Without measuring anything, which two do you think must come out exactly the same size — and why would that have to be true?

agenda.title: I do — Four Ways to Pair Two Angles

content.heading: Position pairs vs. sum pairs
bullets: Adjacent angles share a vertex AND a side (ray), and don't overlap — a position rule.
Vertical angles are the pair directly opposite each other when two lines cross — they share only the vertex, and are always congruent.
Complementary angles are any two angles whose measures add to 90° — position doesn't matter.
Supplementary angles are any two angles whose measures add to 180° — position doesn't matter.
A linear pair is a special case: adjacent angles whose outer sides form a straight line — always supplementary.

agenda.title: Explore — Rotate the Line, Watch All Four

content.heading: Turn one line through the vertex; watch every angle update
content.note: On the sim, drag the gold handle to rotate the second line through the vertex. Watch m∠1–m∠4 update together. Which two angles always stay equal no matter how far you rotate? Which pairs always add to 180°?

content.preview: Build session: hunt real complementary/supplementary pairs and build an "X" to confirm vertical angles are congruent.

success_criteria.text: I can define adjacent, vertical, complementary, and supplementary angles, and spot a linear pair
I can compute an unknown angle's measure given its complementary, supplementary, or vertical partner
I can identify the two pairs of vertical angles made by two intersecting lines and state that they are always congruent

questions.prompt: Two angles that share a vertex and a common side, without overlapping, are called ___ angles.
questions.explain: Adjacent angles share a vertex and one ray, sitting right next to each other with no overlap.

questions.prompt: When two lines cross, the two angles directly opposite the vertex from each other are called…
questions.explain: Vertical angles are the opposite pair formed by two intersecting lines — they share only the vertex, and they are always congruent.

questions.prompt: ∠A and ∠B are complementary. If m∠A = 37°, what is m∠B? Show the subtraction.
questions.explain: Complementary angles' measures always add to 90°, so subtract the known angle from 90°.

questions.prompt: ∠P and ∠Q are supplementary. If m∠P = 118°, what is m∠Q? Show the subtraction.
questions.explain: Supplementary angles' measures always add to 180°, so subtract the known angle from 180°.

questions.prompt: Draw two lines crossing at a single point H. Label the four angles formed ∠1, ∠2, ∠3, ∠4 in order around H, then mark which two pairs are vertical angles.
questions.answer: Two lines crossing at H with four angles labeled in order; ∠1 & ∠3 marked as one vertical pair, ∠2 & ∠4 marked as the other.
questions.explain: Rubric: two clearly crossing lines with four angles labeled in order around H (1 pt) + both vertical pairs (opposite, non-adjacent) correctly identified (1 pt).

questions.prompt: Two lines intersect at H, forming ∠AHC, ∠CHB, ∠BHD, and ∠DHA going around the point. If m∠AHC = 65°, find m∠CHB and name the relationship you used.
questions.answer: m∠CHB = 180° − 65° = 115°, because ∠AHC and ∠CHB are adjacent and form a linear pair (they're supplementary).
questions.explain: ∠AHC and ∠CHB share ray HC, and their outer sides (HA and HB) form a straight line, so their measures must add to 180°.

questions.prompt: Exit: without a protractor, look at any two straight things crossing nearby (scissors, a lowercase 't', a folded corner) and name ONE angle pair you can identify by sight alone — adjacent, vertical, complementary, or supplementary — and explain why.
